I don't particularly like the smell of that either.  I originally started with returning a `Delorean` where the tz was None.  I think it actually passed all unit tests, but figured that that was not legit.  My guess is that having a legit tz is an expectation engrained in all of delorean, and having None would break a lot of ops.  Maybe not?

Maybe an alternative is to drop `naivemode='naive'` and *always* return a `Delorean`, but still have the `naivemode='raise'` option.  The naive `datetime` could be included in the raised exception.  Returning usable data in an exception smells even worse, though.  If just an exception, a subsequent call could then be made to a (new) `delorean.parse_datetime` call which always returns a `datetime`, but that seems like busy work to have to call twice.

Or maybe a whole new call that returns both a `Delorean` representation of the time *and* a datetime representation?

    >>> ret = delorean.parse2('2015-02-04T16:33:21.247513Z') #Z for UTC
    >>> [type(v) for v in ret]
    [<class 'delorean.dates.Delorean'>, <type 'datetime.datetime'>]
    >>> ret = delorean.parse2('2015-02-04T16:33:21.247513Z')  # no tz data
    [<type 'NoneType'>, <type 'datetime.datetime'>]

where the Delorean ret is None when there's no tz data.

That seems overly complex, though.  My current code is simpler, although the return type *is* dependent on an arg value.

Ideally, we should start off with `filter = None` because we expose this now. We can do this right the first time by removing the hardcoded filter and toss in a good example too.
```
Examples:
# show pending tasks
task {
    filter = 'status:pending'
}
```
If we use `task export` without `filter` above, we will get old tasks too. Maybe a limitation on their part, but this gives us more points (or data) for py3status to play with. Defaulting `filter = status:pending` is somewhat like using `\?not_zero` as the completed/deleted tasks will have `0` for `{id}`.

I'm not sure what we can do with old tasks. Maybe a count along with hiding the completed/deleted tasks to display a full count of tasks, not just `pending`.

I think it's probably worth doing a bit more work upfront to parameterize this by creating an actual model for the issue tracker, and then referencing it from here. That will make it easier to support more than one github repository in the future. Something like this in `model/models.py`:

```py
class IssueTracker(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(...)  # e.g. `bugzilla.mozilla.org`, `servo`
    base_url = models.URLField()  # e.g. `https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=`
```

You could then reference it via a foreign key field in this model:

```py
class PerformanceAlertSummary(models.Model):
   ...
   issue_tracker = models.ForeignKeyField(IssueTracker)
```

You can create the initial set of values you want (servo, bugzilla) in the fixtures here:

I guess I was not clear enough.

SystemPart.get() and SystemPart.get_active() do need the Database instance, that's fine. And they call database.select(), so when the database creates the SystemPart instances they will get a value for the _database member - just like any other model instance that is loaded from the database! To clarify - database.select will set _database, it will not be a feature specific to the SystemPart class.

So once you load a SystemPart, it will have a _database and there will not be a need to pass the database to operations that work on that instance (freeze, drop, etc.).

I think that this mechanism (the _database member) could open up additional features that work on instances. Consider for example lazy loading - I will be able to do something like:
`Database.select("SELECT id from MyLazyModel", model_class=MyLazyModel) `
which will load instances that only know their ids, and all the rest of the fields are not loaded. And then I can add an instance method MyLazyModel.populate() which will go to the _database and load all the rest of the fields into the model instance.

I could see the value in that, especially in the case of anon users (in case a logout happened). But I don't know that there's a systematic way (with the current system) on a list view to know the difference between there just being no existing records readable by a user (empty list), and there being no *way* for there to be records of this kind readable by the current user (403). Would love your help in thinking this through -- going to start by laying out a few examples.

- If a DeviceOwner does `GET /api/facility/`, but no facilities exist on the device, we probably don't want to return a 403, just an empty list. It's not that they're not allowed to ask, it's just that there aren't any.
- Similarly, if there are facility users for Facility A but not for Facility B, then when a coach for Facility B does `GET /api/facilityuser/`, it would seem to make sense to return an empty list, not a 403. They have read permission for a subset of facility users, it's just that their subset happens to be empty at the moment.
- The example shown in the test here is where it becomes more of a gray area for me. Similar to the last examples, we are filtering down the queryset to only those objects readable by the current (in this case, anonymous) user. While in this case (and probably the majority of cases) this list would be empty for an anon user, it might not always be -- there could be some models we want to allow an anonymous user to view.

So, I guess we have three main options:
- Always throw a 403 for anonymous requests
- Leave it like it is (on list views, just filter down to a readable subset, which for some users might never be anything but empty)
- Adapt the permissions system to specify table-level (non-object-level) permissions. If we see a strong use case, we can look at what that would take.

Let me know what you think! Thanks!

(Note that requests for a specific object/detail view already throw a 403 if unauthorized; this only applies to list views)

```python
class PyEVMBackend:
```

```python
from eth_tester import EthereumTester, PyEVMBackend
```

OK for the sake of clarity in this PR and for moving forwards (ignoring what has been said/done before now)

Do we:
 1. Support _only_ the Ganga style of writing integration tests which means supporting tests which run in a particular order.
 2. Support _only_ the approach of making every single test 'run'-able by itself without any additional tests having been run?
 3. Support the devs choice in writing the test.
 4. None of the above.

To my mind the 3rd option is supporting the devs choice is the practical situation but this is causing controversy.

My entire concern here is that we're moving goalposts without some discussion.

If a test has been written tested/verified on multiple machines and uploaded then it's a pain to ask for it to be re-written against a stylistic view that was never decided against or agreed upon.
If a test is yet to be written it is much easier to start against a given mindset and write the test that needs writing. Porting a test introduces many more hidden problems and concerns me much more.

As with all the code I write I'm happy for someone to come along and replace it with a more standards complaint version or whatever version suits whatever problem you're having but I'm putting in the code to solve the problem(s) I'm having right now as I try to write new features.

to something like:

```python
from os.path import abspath, expandvars, expanduser, normcase
def iexpandfilenames(filenames, force=False, ignore_empty=True):
    if not isinstance(filenames, Iterable):
        filenames = [filenames]

    for f in filenames:
        sanitised_path = abspath(normcase(expandvars(expanduser(f.strip()))))
        if ignore_empty:
            continue
        if os.path.exists(sanitised_path) or force:
            yield sanitised_path
        else:
            getLogger().debug("Filename: %s doesn't exist using it anyway" % f.strip())
            yield f.strip()

def expandfilenames(filenames, force=False):
    return [f for f in iexpandfilenames(filenames, force)]
```

The cache is actually not for performance reasons, it's to be able to start keeping around within-run information (namely `ssl_copy`). For a while, it's been bothering me that we re-parse and find server blocks anew every time, even when we want to have found the same server block, instead of just keeping a reference to it. But we couldn't do that before because `get_vhosts()` was ephemeral. I think that this is a cleaner, safer way to be doing it generally.

If we didn't cache them, we'd certainly have to change the `use_if` as you mentioned above.

But, that being said, the way for users of this class to create vhosts is to call this method, which handles updating the cache. They'd also have to update the raw blocks, which they also wouldn't know to do. I think adding a comment saying "this method is the only way to create a new vhost" would suffice.

#### LE_PYTHON affecting bootstrap selection

I'm unsure if we want to be taking `LE_PYTHON` set by the user into account when determining the bootstrap script to use.

My main reason for thinking this is the bootstrap script changing based on the user's environment can lead to some strange/obnoxious behavior. For example, you're running certbot-auto in cron as root and you get root email that says that certbot-auto needs to be run interactively to get permission to install new packages. You log in as your normal user and run certbot-auto. If you have `LE_PYTHON` set in one environment and not the other, a different bootstrap script may be selected in the two environments. This is similar to the issues we were having when there wasn't one Certbot install location on the system and it depended on the user's home directory.

If `LE_PYTHON` is set for the non-root user, it may not rebootstrap despite certbot-auto saying you have to. If `LE_PYTHON` is set for the root user, you may rebootstrap when running as a non-root user, but when certbot-auto is run again from cron, a different bootstrap script is selected so certbot-auto thinks you have to rebootstrap again!

If we do this, I think the only downside is we may install packages we may end up not needing, but doing this for the very few users who have been setting `LE_PYTHON` seems like it might be less harmful, confusing, and complex than the above behavior. If users know nothing else needs to be installed, there's a `--no-bootstrap` flag.

#### Overwriting LE_PYTHON

When setting `LE_PYTHON` here though, I think we should respect the value the user set it to and not just overwrite it. Setting `LE_PYTHON=python2.6` and having it suddenly run in Python 3 is a little confusing and unexpected. I think we should only set this value to `python3` if it wasn't set by the user.

My thought here is in the next release, you'll still be able to force Certbot to use Python 2.6 if you set `LE_PYTHON`. In the next release, we'll also start printing deprecation warnings if you're running in Python 2.6. In a subsequent release, we'll update `DeterminePythonVersion` to complain about your entombed Python version if it's less than 27 which should force you to stop using 2.6 entirely.

I don't think this is what happens: browser hard refresh does not force server side cache flush and shouldn't (unless I misunderstood).
HTTP headers says to the browser to keep cache and it won't perform any other request on this URL unless TTL is expired, even if the server-side cache is flushed.
Client side can't see applicative cache and, most of the time, it's unrelated:
- some entries are already cached and this create a double entry (in fact `n+1` where `n` is the combination of extras unkown query strings)
- Static API entries (reference list, translations...), like reference lists, that are faster that an applicative cache access will be slower because it will trigger a cache rountrip, but these entries are typically those we want to cache on the HTTP part
- Caching the full reponse instead of the data means caching any session/user related data that are embedded in it (headers/cookies...) => exploit
- caching the full response does not benefit from/to other cached data access

This is why I think it should focus on doing proper HTTP caching and let the applicative cache part to Flask-Caching which allows more fine tuned server side caching.
If we want to cache responses, this require something more complex to be secure and performant, either something like Varnish if we want to handle it ourself or something like Cloudflare if we want an external service.
